items.q: What absorber is needed to stop beta radiation?
items.correct: A few millimetres of aluminium (or similar thin metal sheet).
items.misconception: A sheet of paper — beta is only slightly more penetrating than alpha.
items.random_wrong: Thick lead — only lead can stop any radiation.
items.why: Students remember 'paper, aluminium, lead' as a trio but mix up which goes with which radiation type.

items.q: Is radioactive decay periodic — does each nucleus decay at regular intervals?
items.correct: No — decay is a random process; half-life is a statistical property of a large population.
items.misconception: Yes — each nucleus 'ticks' at intervals of one half-life until it decays.
items.random_wrong: Yes — half-life is the time between each emission from the source.
items.why: Students treat half-life as a clock for individual atoms instead of a probability-based property of bulk samples.

items.q: Is background radiation always present, even when no source is being studied?
items.correct: Yes — natural sources (rocks, cosmic rays, food) produce a low level of radiation everywhere on Earth.
items.misconception: No — background radiation only appears near nuclear power stations.
items.random_wrong: Yes, but only in cities with old buildings.
items.why: Students associate radiation exclusively with nuclear technology and don't realise natural background dose is ubiquitous.
The answer examiners want is: alpha. “beta” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
Correct answer: any isotope at any temperature. “isotopes at high T only” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Half-life 10 min, starting at 800 Bq. Activity (Bq) after 30 min?
items.why: Exact answer: 100. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
